chunk_summary: Obesity is a major risk factor for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), requiring urgent screening for visual symptoms and intracranial pressure to prevent permanent vision loss.
learning_objectives: Recognize headache and visual symptoms indicative of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) in obese patients.
Understand the urgency of ophthalmology and neuroimaging assessments in suspected IIH cases.
Learn the diagnostic steps, including fundoscopy, MRI/MRV, and lumbar puncture, for IIH evaluation.
Identify emergency referral criteria for IIH presentations with severe symptoms.
key_facts: Obesity is a significant risk factor for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH).
Suspect IIH in adolescents with headache plus visual symptoms like diplopia, pulsatile tinnitus, or papilledema.
MRI brain with venography (MRV) is preferred for imaging to exclude mass lesions and venous sinus thrombosis.
Lumbar puncture for opening pressure and CSF studies is performed after imaging in specialist settings.
Emergency referral is required for altered sensorium, focal neurologic signs, or rapidly progressive visual symptoms.
Avoid empiric acetazolamide in primary care unless imaging and ophthalmology are imminent.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Consensus recommendations emphasize urgent ophthalmology and neuroimaging for suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension to prevent permanent visual loss. (https://adc.bmj.com/content/109/2/165)
document_key_topics: Urgent screening and management of visual symptoms
Diagnostic pathways: imaging and lumbar puncture
Emergency referral criteria for severe IIH presentations
